coredata.dc:title: Detection of corrosion inhibition by dithiane self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) on copper
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Li
Zheng, Siyuan
Zhu, Hailin
Ma, Xuemei
Hu, Zhiyong
coredata.dc:description:
Background Metal corrosion leads to serious economic energy loss. It is urgent to develop high-efficiency and environmental protection corrosion inhibitors.
Methods By simple chemical synthesis, 2‑butyl‑1,3-dithiane (BD) and 2-heptyl-1,3-dithiane (HD) corrosion inhibitors were synthesized. These two molecules formed protective self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) on the copper surface in ethanol solution.
Significant findings The electrochemical tests and surface morphology tests suggested that the inhibition performance of BD was better than HD for copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium with the maximal inhibition efficiency of 99.6% for BD-SAMs and 98.6% for HD-SAMs, which may be due to the greater steric hindrance of HD. Certainly, the inhibition efficiency was also significantly influenced by concentration and self-assembly time. Compared with two self-assembled molecules, the hydrophobicity of the copper surface with BD layer was enhanced, resulting in the promotion of anti-corrosion efficiency. Additionally, XPS analysis reveals that the SAMs prevent copper corrosion by controlling the production of Cu2+, which is the decisive step in the corrosion process. Furthermore, quantum chemical calculation and molecular dynamics simulation provide evidence for the inhibition efficiency and the mechanism of the target molecules, and the conclusion is consistent with the experimental results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-assemble monolayers
Copper
Corrosion inhibition
Dithiane derivatives
Theoretical study

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of self-assembly concentration on inhibition
Influence of self-assembly time on inhibition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of pure targets and SAMs.

XPS survey spectra and high-resolution spectra, (a)XPS survey spectra for the blank, BD-SAMs and HD-SAMs; (b)typical XPS spectra of Cu2p for the blank, BD-SAMs and HD-SAMs; (c) high-resolution XPS spectra of S2pfor the BD-SAMs; (d) high-resolution XPS spectra of S2p for the HD-SAMs.
The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, Cu2p3/2 and O1s for the blank, BD-SAMs and HD-SAMs.
Nyquist, Bode and polarizationplots of BD-SAMs (a, c, e) and HD-SAMs (b, d, f) with different self-assembly concentrations at 298 K.
The equivalent circuit with (a) and without (b) Warburg impedance used to fit the electrochemical impedance data.
Nquist, Bode and polarizationplots of BD-SAMs (a,c,e) and HD-SAMs (b,d,f) with different self-assembly time at 298 K.

The SEM surface morphology and contact angle pictures of copper samples with and without SAMs, (a) without corrosion; (b) corrosion without SAMs; (c) corrosion with BD-SAMs; (d) corrosion with HD-SAMs.
Molecular frontier orbital diagram of BD and HD molecular.
Side and top views of molecular dynamics adsorption simulations of self-assembled molecules on copper surfaces.
The atomic percentage for the blank, BD-SAMs and BD-SAMs from XPS survey spectra.
The relevant data of EIS with different self-assembly concentrations at 298 K.
The relevant data of polarization curves with different self-assembly concentrations at 298 K.
The relevant data of EIS with different self-assembly time at 298 K.
The relevant data of polarization curves with different self-assembly time at 298 K.

head.ce:title: Detection of corrosion inhibition by dithiane self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) on copper

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dezhou Graduate School, North University of China, Dezhou, Shandong 253034, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDezhou Graduate School, North University of China, Dezhou, Shandong 253034, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal corrosion leads to serious economic energy loss. It is urgent to develop high-efficiency and environmental protection corrosion inhibitors.

By simple chemical synthesis, 2‑butyl‑1,3-dithiane (BD) and 2-heptyl-1,3-dithiane (HD) corrosion inhibitors were synthesized. These two molecules formed protective self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) on the copper surface in ethanol solution.

The electrochemical tests and surface morphology tests suggested that the inhibition performance of BD was better than HD for copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium with the maximal inhibition efficiency of 99.6% for BD-SAMs and 98.6% for HD-SAMs, which may be due to the greater steric hindrance of HD. Certainly, the inhibition efficiency was also significantly influenced by concentration and self-assembly time. Compared with two self-assembled molecules, the hydrophobicity of the copper surface with BD layer was enhanced, resulting in the promotion of anti-corrosion efficiency. Additionally, XPS analysis reveals that the SAMs prevent copper corrosion by controlling the production of Cu2+, which is the decisive step in the corrosion process. Furthermore, quantum chemical calculation and molecular dynamics simulation provide evidence for the inhibition efficiency and the mechanism of the target molecules, and the conclusion is consistent with the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper and its alloys are utilized in various fields such as aerospace, automobile manufacturing, and bridge construction [1–4]. However, they are susceptible to corrosion from the surrounding medium, which can cause great economic and energy losses, and even major accidents [5–10]. The research on metal corrosion protection has been a hot topic [11–15]. Metal corrosion protection methods include substrate change methods (such as alloys), electrochemical methods (such as cathodic protection) [16,17], coatings [18,19] and medium change methods (adding corrosion inhibitor) [20–23] to delay or prevent metal corrosion. Above all, adding corrosion inhibitors is simple, efficient and easy to operate. It has become one of the most important methods for metal corrosion protection, especially in the fields of pickling and petrochemicals [24–26].

Generally, traditional high-efficiency corrosion inhibitors are heteroatomic (S, O, N) organic compounds containing lone pairs electrons, such as triazole, imidazole, thiophene and so on [27–30]. These electron-rich organic compounds can donate electrons to the d orbital of the metal and then adsorb on the metal surface to form a protective film [31,32]. Considering that organics are not environmentally friendly, it is necessary to develop green and efficient corrosion inhibitors [13,33–36]. At present, there are many kinds of organic corrosion inhibitors and the structure-activity relationship is complicated [37–39], which makes the design and screening of corrosion inhibitors difficult. Additionally, it is difficult to recover and reuse the corrosion inhibitor in the later stage due to the complex composition of the corrosive medium. This not only consumes a lot of energy but also causes a loss of economic energy.

As a matter of fact, the self-assembly technology of non-corrosive media can change this situation to some extent. Self-assembly refers to a technique in which basic structural units (molecules, nanomaterials, microns or larger scales) spontaneously form ordered structures [40]. In the process of self-assembly, basic structural units spontaneously organize or aggregate into a stable structure with regular geometric appearance under the interaction based on non-covalent bonds [41,42]. Some researchers have applied self-assembly technology to metal corrosion protection [43–45]. Huang's group designed ionic polymers (IPs) aggregates with cular-like shapes [46]. The prepared target IPs may self-assemble the orderly aggregates on the copper surface by the chemical coordination with metal, performing excellent corrosion inhibition efficiency in aggressive H2SO4 aqueous solution. The investigation demonstrated the self-assembly of nano/micro-amphiphilic dendrimer aggregated based on strong π-πinteraction for protecting copper in NaCl solution. Furthermore, Chen's group designed self-assembled monolayers of thiazoles incorporating hydrocarbon and fluorocarbon chains on copper substrate [47]. The results showed that the protection ability of SAMs depended on the concentration, time and structure of self-assemble molecules. Although the application of self-assembly technology in the field of metal corrosion protection solves the problem that some organic compounds are difficult to dissolve in aqueous solution and improves the corrosion inhibition efficiency. Nevertheless, the structure-activity relationship between self-assembled molecules and corrosion inhibition efficiency remains to be further studied. In order to better understand the corrosion and protection of metals, the differences and connections between self-assembled molecules and traditional corrosion inhibitors should be further explored.

In this study, two thioether compounds with different substituents were designed and synthesized as target self-assembled molecules. Promisingly, self-assembly molecules can be recycled by removing the solvent through rotary evaporation due to the volatility of ethanol as a solvent. The inhibitory effects of SAMs on copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution were tested. Moreover, the structure-activity relationship between the self-assembled molecular structure and the corrosion inhibition efficiency was further explored. The inhibition mechanism of self-assembled molecules was analyzed by combining surface analysis techniques (such as SEM and XPS) and theoretical calculations. This research has laid a theoretical foundation for the design and development of new self-assembled molecules in the future.

The sulfuric acid was purchased from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Industry (China) to prepare 0.5 M sulfuric acid solution. Anhydrous ethanol (99.7%) was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, both of which were of analytical purity grade. The copper sheet used in this study was pure copper (99.9% ≤ Cu). Copper sample was cut into 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.1 cm and 1 × 1 × 0.1 cm for a series of surface morphology and composition analysis. Prior to each test, the samples were polished by abrasive paper, washed and drying, then immersed in inhibitor ethanol solution forming SAMs. After this, it was transferred to the corrosive solution (0.5 M sulfuric acid solution), cleaned with ultrapure water, then dried successively to observe the morphology immediately. The samples for electrochemical tests were polished and sealed in epoxy resin to expose only 1 cm2 of the working area. The corrosion solution was 0.5 M sulfuric acid with different concentrations of targets.

The synthesis routes of inhibitors molecules were shown in Fig. 1 . A 250 mL round-bottomed flask was placed in the dark before adding NBS (2.36 g, 0.095 equivalents) to a stirred solution of alkyl aldehyde (10.0 mL) and 1,3-Dimercaptopropane (15.98 mL) at room temperature. After 20 min, the mixture was partitioned between Et2O (50 mL) and H2O (50 mL). Finally, the yellowy liquids products were obtained. At room temperature (298 K), Bruker nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) apparatus (400 MHz) was used to identify the chemical structures of targets. Based on the chemical shifts of tetramethylsilane (TMS), the 1H or 13C NMR peaks were analyzed. Fig. 2 displayed the 1H NMR spectrum of target molecules. The corresponding 13C NMR spectrum was shown in the Supporting Information.

The object of study is pure copper which the content is higher than 99.9%. Before the experiment, the metal copper was polished with different grades of shaper, cleaned with ethanol and dried at room temperature. Then, the copper samples were immersed in ethanol solution containing different concentrations of target inhibitors at various time for the self-assembly process. The samples treated without corrosion inhibitor were used as the blank group.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi, Al Kα X-ray source) and Fourier transform infrared reflection spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50, America) were used to detect the information of self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) on copper surface. Metal samples were analyzed by XPS at room temperature in a K-Alpha XPS apparatus (Thermal Fisher, USA). And the XPS detection angle was 90° The C1s (284.8 eV) was used to calibrate all peaks by the Avantage software. Before each test, the copper samples with the dimension of 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm were polished with different grades (#400 - #7000) emery paper, ultrasonically cleaned by ethanol, and dried at room temperature, then immersed in target inhibitor ethanol solution under optimal self-assembly conditions (10 mM, 18 h) at 298 K. After self-assembling, samples were cleaned slightly with ultrapure water and dried by vacuum. The samples were then immersed in a corrosive medium (0.5 M H2SO4) for 24 h before being taken out, cleaned and dried for use. For FT-IR, the pure powders were used to compare with SAMs. Accordingly, the XPS of the blank was analyzed to deduce the interaction between inhibitor molecules and copper substrate.

Electrochemical testing was a common method of measuring metal corrosion. A three-electrode system was applied for electrochemical testing by CHI 760E electrochemical workstation. The saturated calomel electrode was the reference electrode, the platinum plate electrode was the counter electrode, and the copper sample with and without SAMs was the working electrode. First, the open circuit potential (E ocp) test was carried out to make the system reach a stable state. Then, electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) and polarization curves were successively used to investigate the corrosion inhibition performance of SAMs. The E ocp was used as the initial potential for EIS measurement. And the frequency range was 10−2–105 Hz. Impedance data were fitted and analyzed with the appropriate equivalent circuit by ZSimpWin software. Finally, the polarization curves were measured in the +250 mV and −250 mV range of the initial potential with the sweep rate of 1 mV/s. The polarization curve data were obtained by linear extrapolation. The influence of concentration and time of self-assemble on inhibition efficiency was further studied. All experiments were repeated three times to obtain good reproducibility. The corrosion inhibition efficiencies were calculated by following equations:
(1)
η =
R p
−
R
p , 0
R p
×
100 %
(2)
η =
(
i
c o r r , 0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r , 0
)
×
100 %

Here, the R p and R p,0 were the polarization resistance in the presence and absence of inhibitors, respectively. The i corr,0 (without inhibitors) and i corr (with inhibitors) were corrosion current densities [48].

The scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL-JSM-7800F, Japan) and contact angle (CA, XG-CAMB3, China) tests were used to further explore the corrosion information of copper surface with and without SAMs. The acceleration voltage of the SEM measurement is 15.0 kV. Before each test, the copper samples with and without SAMs (optimal self-assembly conditions:10 mM, 18 h) were corroded in 0.5 M H2SO4 medium for 24 h at 298 K. Then the samples were removed, cleaned slightly with ultrapure water and dried by vacuum. The sample without SAMs as the blank was used for comparison.

In theory, the quantum chemical computation and the molecular dynamic simulation (Material Studio 7.0, Accelrys Inc) were performed to investigate the dynamic process of self-assemble molecules on copper surface. The reaction center can also be inferred from the electron cloud distribution. And the B3LYP method of Density Functional Theory (DFT) is applied to perform geometric optimization and frequency calculations of the structures of two thioether compounds using the 6311+ G (d, p) group. The molecular frontier orbital and related parameters were obtained including the energy of highest occupied molecular orbital (E HOMO), the energy of lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (E LUMO), the dipole moment (µ), and energy gap (ΔE). The molecular dynamics simulations are performed using the Forcite module of the Material Studio 7.0 software. First, a Cu (111) crystal surface was chosen as the object of study to build 5 × 5 Cu crystals, which were fixed in a box with periodic boundary conditions, and then one SAMs molecule and 300 ethanol molecules were introduced. The kinetic adsorption of SAMs molecules on the metal surface was simulated using the COMPASS force field and NVT system with a simulation step of 1 fs, time of 1000 ps, and temperature of 298 K, in aqueous phase. As a result, the adsorption model and adsorption energy (E Cu-Inhibitor) were obtained and analyzed. The relevant formulations are shown below:
(3)
Δ
E =
E LUMO
−
E HOMO
(4)
E
Cu − Inhibitor
=
E Total
−
E Inhibitor
−
E Cu
Here, the E Total, E Inhibitor and E Cu are the energy of total system, inhibitor and copper substrate, respectively.

The comparison of FTIR spectra of as-synthesized SAMs with pure powders are displayed in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3, the wavelength at 2928.6 cm−1 and 2855.7 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CH in -CH2 [49]. The peak at 1465 ± 20 cm−1 is the deformation vibration of CH in -CH2 or the asymmetric deformation vibration of CH in -CH3. These characteristic peaks are present in the corresponding SAMs samples, indicating that the target molecule is assembled efficaciously on the copper surface. Besides, the peak position between 900 and 1300 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of C-S-C [50]. For FT-IR tests, the characteristic peak may not be detected when the content is very low. Therefore, the peaks of pure target powder and SAMs do not correspond exactly one to one.

Fig. 4 is the XPS survey spectra and Cu2p high-resolution spectra of the blank, BD-SAMs and HD-SAMs samples. The corresponding atomic percentage is listed in Table 1 . Other high-resolution spectra including C1s, Cu2p and O1s results are shown in Fig. 5 . Obvious difference can be found between the blank and the two SAMs samples. That is, the S element is only detected on two SAMs samples. Thus, S elements are mainly analyzed in Fig. 4 to reveal the relationship between investigated targets and the copper surface.

As can be observed from the XPS survey spectra, Cu, C, N and O elements are detected on all samples. Particularly, the increase in C content on the copper surface with SAMs attached, whereas the content of Cu and O gradually decreases compared with the blank from the Table 1, which is attributed to the self-assembly of target molecules on the copper surface. Moreover, S element is only on the SAMs samples, which further suggesting the successful self-assemble of target molecules. Indeed, the XPS is a surface analysis technique with a testing depth of about 1–10 nm. Changes in element content can confirm whether the self-assembled molecules successfully self-assemble to form a protective film on the metal surface.

In the XPS Cu2p high resolution spectrum, due to the serious corrosion in the corrosive medium, there are obvious Cu (Ⅱ) satellite peaks in the blank sample, but there are no Cu (Ⅱ) satellite peaks in the two SAMs samples. This fully demonstrates that the self-assembled molecules can effectively inhibit the corrosion of copper. In acidic media, copper is first oxidized quickly to Cu (Ⅰ). Then Cu (Ⅰ) is further slowly oxidized to Cu (Ⅱ), which is decisive step of copper corrosion [51]. It can be concluded that SAMs effectively inhibits copper corrosion by controlling the decisive step of copper corrosion reaction.

In the XPS S2p high-resolution spectra of two SAMs, the peak at 162.01 eV (BD-SAMs)/161.76 eV (HD-SAMs) and 163.67 eV (BD-SAMs)/163.44 eV (HD-SAMs) can be ascribed to S-C2p3/2 and S-C2p1/2, respectively. The higher peaks at 162.65 eV (BD-SAMs)/162.42 eV (HD-SAMs) and 164.76 (BD-SAMs)/164.56 (HD-SAMs) correspond to S-Cu2p3/2 and S-Cu2p1/2 [52]. There are both S-C bonds and S-Cu bonds, indicating that the target molecule self-assemble successfully on the copper surface through chemical process.

The de-convolution of the C1s spectra for the blank produces three peaks with sub-peaks of CC (284.72 eV), COC (286.04 eV) and OCO (288.19 eV), which is owing to the adventitious carbon contamination. It is comprehensible that all samples are exposed to the atmosphere. Bonding energy is related to electron cloud density as well as the electronegativity. The value of electronegativity of H (2.10) is higher than C (2.50) and S (2.44). Therefore, the electron cloud density on C connected to H is the highest, so the bonding energy corresponding to the CH bond is the lowest value. Clearly, the peak at 284.84 eV for BD-SAMs and 284.86 eV for HD-SAMs can be resolved into CC/CH components. The peaks located at 285.87 eV (BD-SAMs) and 285.12 eV (HD-SAMs) are C-S, which suggests the self-assembly of the target molecule on the copper surface [53].

In the de-convolution of Cu2p3/2, peaks of Cu(0)/Cu2(Ⅰ)O and Cu(Ⅱ)O are detected for blank samples, while only peaks of Cu(0)/Cu2(Ⅰ)O are detected for BD-SAMS and HD-SAMS samples, indicating that the SAMs has a supper protective effect. In 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, copper is first oxidized rapidly to cuprous ions (Cu+). Under corrosion, cuprous ions are further oxidized to cupric ions (Cu2+). In this process, the cuprous ion is oxidized to cupric ion this step is slow reaction, which is the decisive step of corrosion reaction [54]. In other words, the SAMs can effectively inhibit the corrosion of copper in acidic media by controlling the decisive step of corrosion reaction.

On the high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s, all samples have three components: Cu(0)/Cu2(Ⅰ)O, OCC and CO-C. As mentioned above, the peaks of OCC and CO-C are caused by the adventitious contamination. It is worth noting that there is a marked difference in their relative percentage content. Compared with blank samples, the content of Cu(0)/Cu2(Ⅰ)O on the surface of samples containing SAMs is significantly reduced, which on the one hand indicates the adsorption of self-assembled molecules on the copper surface to form films, and on the other hand suggests that the oxidation of copper is inhibited.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of self-assembly concentration on inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 is the electrochemical results (Nyquist, Bode and polarization curves tests) of various self-assembly concentrations for copper electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at 298 K, with the self-assembly time of 18 h. The equivalent circuit used to fit the electrochemical impedance spectrum is shown in Fig. 7 . And the related impedance and polarization curve data are listed in Tables 2 and 3 , respectively. In Table 2, the n is the dispersion effect index, and the CPE is pure capacitance with n = 1, Warburg impedance with n = 0.5, pure resistance with n = 0, and inductance with n = −1.

As shown in Fig. 6 (a1,a2), the impedance of blank is composed of capacitive reactance arc in high frequency region and Warburg impedance in low frequency region. However, no obvious Warburg impedance is observed for the SAMs samples, indicating that charge transfer is the control step of the electrochemistry process [55]. The radius of capacitive arc increases with the increase of self-assembly concentration, which demonstrates that the charge transfer step is effectively suppressed and the corrosion of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution is reduced. When the self-assembly concentration is 10 mM, the corresponding capacitive arc radius is the largest, that is, the inhibition efficiency is the largest at the self-assembly concentration of 10 mM. It can be observed from Bode plots (Fig. 6 (b1,b2)) that the value of impedance mode increases and the phase angle becomes wider distinctly with the increase of self-assembly concentration, which is attributed to the film formation of SAMs on copper surface. The denser or thicker the self-assembly film on the copper surface as the increase of self-assembly concentration, the better the corrosion inhibition efficiency. What's more, the variation range of BD is larger than that of HD, which confirms that BD shows better corrosion inhibitor efficiency than HD. In Table 2, the charge transfer resistance (R ct), polarization resistance (R p) and inhibition efficiency (ƞE ) gradually increase with the addition of self-assembly concentration, showing excellent corrosion inhibition performance. When the self-assembly concentration is 10 mM, the inhibition efficiency of BD and HD reaches 99.6% and 98.6%, respectively. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of the thioether SAMs prepared in this experiment was higher than the 80.34% of AYSEL YURT previously studied 1-aminohexylphosphonic acid as SAMs [56]. These results fully reflect that the two target molecules have excellent corrosion inhibition efficiency for copper electrode.

It also can be observed from the polarization curves (Fig. 6(c1,c2)) that the current density decreases dramatically with the increase of self-assembly concentration, indicating that self-assemble molecules can prevent the copper corrosion effectively. The decrease degree of BD is greater than that of HD, which suggests that BD has better corrosion protection ability. The current density decreases in both cathode and anode regions, indicating that the two self-assembled molecules are mixed corrosion inhibitors [57,58]. Compared with blank, the corrosion potential (E corr) values of the SAMs samples move to a negative direction, presenting that the existence of self-assembled film increases the degree of cathodic polarization of corrosion reaction, which is helpful for the corrosion inhibition of copper electrode. In theory, the increase of both cathode polarization and anode polarization is beneficial to the corrosion protection of copper. In Table 5, with the increase of self-assembly concentration, the corrosion current density (i corr) decreases and the corrosion inhibitor efficiency (ƞT ) increases. At the same time, values of cathode slope (β c) and anode slope (β a) also decreased with the addition of concentration since the inhibition of copper corrosion. When the concentration is 10 mM, the inhibition efficiency of two self-assembled molecules is the highest, which is consistent with the conclusion of electrochemical impedance. Likewise, with the increase of target concentration, the thickness and compactness of the SAMs increase and the inhibition efficiency improves accordingly. Compared with BD, the anode branch on the polarization curve of HD has a sharp current surge point, indicating that the BD-SAMs has obvious desorption under this potential, which further verifies that the BD-SAMs is more stable and has potential for better corrosion inhibition performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of self-assembly time on inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 shows impedance and polarization results of copper electrode with different self-assembly time in 0.5 M H2SO4 corrosion solution to research the influence of self-assembly time on inhibition efficiency. All concentration of self-assemble process is controlled to 10 mM. And the impedance and polarization curve data are listed in Tables 4 and 5 .

In contrast to the blank, the Nyquist plots (Fig. 8(a,b)) of SAMs consists only of a flattened capacitive arc without Warburg impedance. The existence of SAMs makes Warburg impedance disappears, which shows that the control step of the electrochemical process is charge transfer process. With the extension of self-assembly time, the radius of capacitive reactance arc increases. In other words, the charge transfer resistance (R ct) increases, indicating that the corrosion of copper electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 corrosion solution is effectively inhibited. It can be seen that the inhibition efficiency of BD-SAMs is better than HD-SAMs. When the self-assembly time is 18 h, the inhibition efficiency of SAMs is the largest, which confirms that 18 h is the best self-assembly time. Extending the self-assembly time to 24 h, the inhibition efficiency decreases slightly, which may be caused by slight desorption of the SAMs over time. The same conclusion can be drawn from Bode plots (Fig. 8(c,d)). In Table 4, the polarization resistance (R p) and inhibition efficiency (ƞE ) increase with the addition of self-assembly time. When the self-assembly time is 18 h, the maximum inhibition efficiency values of BD and HD are 99.6% and 98.9%, respectively. As the self-assembly time continued to increase to 24 h, there is no increase in inhibition efficiency. The explanation is as follows. The self-assembly process of SAMs is a process of adsorption, rearrangement and stabilization. Each step will take a certain amount of time. And only after reaching the stable state after rearrangement, the SAMs is the densest and completest. As time goes on, the self-assembled molecules may undergo slight desorption and then rearrange to a new stable state. In that case, the adsorption and desorption of self-assembled molecules is a dynamic equilibrium process.

As shown in the polarization curves (Fig. 8(e,f)), the existence of SAMs causes the corrosion potential (E corr) of copper electrode to shift negatively and the current density (i corr) to decrease, suggesting the excellent inhibition performance of SAMs. The corrosion inhibition of SAMs is both in the cathode and anode regions, which indicates that the target molecule is a mixed inhibitor. Besides, the values of current density (i corr) decrease compared with the blank, which demonstrates that the SAMs can effectively inhibit the corrosion of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 media. Furthermore, the shift range of E corr is less than 85 mV, showing that the self-assembled molecules belong to mixed corrosion inhibitors [59]. The SAMS can not only inhibit the dissolution of anode copper but also reduce the reaction of cathode hydrogen evolution. At the self-assembly concentration of 18 h, the maximum inhibition efficiency is 99.0% and 98.1%, respectively. And the inhibition efficiency of BD is better than HD. This conclusion is consistent with the previous conclusion.
Fig. 9 is the SEM surface appearance and corresponding contact angle pictures of copper samples with and without SAMs.

Comparison of the samples before and after immersion in corrosive media shows that the surface of the copper without immersion in corrosive media is very smooth, with only slight scratches from grinding. After corrosion immersion, the surface roughness increases on copper surface and there are many corrosion pits, which are derived from the corrosion of the corrosive medium. The surface morphology of the copper has a significant change after self-assemble treatment. The existence of SAMs makes the sample surface smoother. In other words, the SAMs can effectively inhibit the corrosion of copper in sulfuric acid solution. In addition, BD-SAMs has a more efficient protective effect than HD-SAMs, which is consistent with the electrochemical results. Contact angle further provides evidence for corrosion inhibition of self-assembled molecules. The successful self-assembly of the self-assembled membrane is further confirmed. Furthermore, the values of contact angles rise from 57.7° (blank) to103.2° (BD-SAMs) and 93.0° (HD-SAMs) in Fig. 9. It demonstrates that the hydrophobicity of copper surface is enhanced owing to the existence of SAMs. The enhanced hydrophobicity makes it difficult for corrosive substances in solution to get close to the copper surface, which is beneficial to the corrosion protection of copper.

More information is obtained by theoretical calculation. Figs. 10
and 11 show the frontier orbital diagrams of target molecules and molecular dynamics adsorption simulation diagrams of self-assembled molecules on metal surfaces, respectively.

The HOMO-LUMO level is referred to as the frontier orbital, representing the highest occupied molecular orbital and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. It's clear in Fig. 10 that the HOMO and LUMO are centered on the dithiane molecular ring, implying that the dithiane ring is the active center for self-assembly. Naturally, the energy of HOMO (E HOMO) and LUMO (E LUMO) is related to a molecule's ability to donate or gain electrons. E HOMO is higher, which means they're more likely to donate electrons. The lower the E LUMO, the easier it is to gain electrons. The E HOMO of BD (−0.209763 eV) is greater than that of HD (−0.212129 eV), which demonstrates that BD is easier to donate electrons to the empty d orbital of copper for self-assembly than HD. And HD (−0.003776 eV) has a lower E LUMO value than BD (−0.002668 eV), indicating that HD is easier to gain electrons than BD. Besides, the lower energy gap (ΔE, 0.207095 eV for BD, 0.208353 eV for HD,) value and larger dipole moment (μ, 2.43 Debye for BD, 2.36 Debye for HD) also represent higher reactivity [60,61]. In conclusion, BD with higher reactivity is easier to donate electrons than HD in theory, indicating that BD has higher reactivity for self-assembly on the copper surface performing a better corrosion inhibition.

As can be seen from Fig. 11, the adsorption configuration of the target molecule on the copper surface is parallel to the heterocyclic plane, and the substituted alkyl chain is at a certain angle to the copper surface. This is consistent with the conclusion of quantum chemistry that heterocyclic ring is the active center of the reaction. HD has a longer alkyl chain, whose alkyl chain appears at a certain angle to the adsorption plane. Longer alkyl chains may hinder the adsorption and assembly of other HD molecules, showing a weaker corrosion inhibition. Furthermore, lower adsorption energy (E Inhibitor-Cu) suggests stronger adsorption [62]. The adsorption energy of BD (−269.44 kJ/mol) is lower than that of HD (−167.02 kJ/mol), which further indicates that BD is easier to adsorb on the copper surface spontaneously, showing potentially excellent corrosion inhibition. The results agree with the previous experiments.

Molecular self-assembly is a process of molecular rearrangement under the condition of thermodynamic equilibrium, which is influenced by various factors such as concentration, self-assembly time and so on. This process involves the adsorption, rearrangement and stabilization of self-assembled molecules. In this work, the optimal self-assembly concentration is 10 mM and the self-assembly time is 18 h. As time continues to extend, the self-assembled molecules may be slightly detached, showing no increase in inhibition efficiency. In other words, the self-assembled process reaches a stable state in 18 h. Target molecules self-assemble onto the surface of copper by chemical adsorption to form a protective layer. Theoretically, BD with stronger donated effect is easier to self-assemble on copper surface than HD, showing better corrosion inhibition effect. It may be that the large steric hindrance of HD is not conducive to the self-assembly process. Thus, the results show that the inhibition effect of BD-SAMS is better than that of HD-SAMS. In acid solution, the copper is preferentially oxidized to cuprous ion (Cu+), and then the cuprous ion is further oxidized to cupric ion (Cu2+), which is the decisive step. SAMs effectively protects copper from corrosion by inhibiting the formation of cupric ion (Cu2+). In other words, the decisive step of the corrosion reaction is inhibited.

Two dithiane derivatives were successfully designed and synthesized. The inhibition effect and mechanism of BD-SAMs and HD-SAMs on copper surface in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, and drew the following conclusions:
(1)
BD and HD can successfully self-assemble to copper surfaces at 298 K with an optimal self-assembly time of 18 h at a concentration of 10 mM.
(2)
BD-SAMs have better inhibition efficiency than HD-SAMs, with the maximum inhibition efficiency of about 99%.
(3)
Both target molecules act as mixed corrosion inhibitors.
(4)
The SAMs inhibit copper corrosion by controlling the formation of Cu2+, which is the decisive step of the corrosion reaction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: I would like to declare on behalf of my co-authors that the work described was original research that has not been published previously, and not under consideration for publication elsewhere. All the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed. To the best of our knowledge, the named authors have no conflict of interest, financial or otherwise.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No.20210302124337, 202103021224207).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2022.104610.
